abyssal: Relating to or denoting the depths or bed of the ocean, especially between about 10,000 and 20,000 feet down.
anatomy: The branch of science concerned with the bodily structure of humans, animals, and other living organisms.
benthic: Relating to or occurring at the bottom of a body of water
catalyst: A substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without itself undergoing any permanent chemical change.
dendrite: A short branched extension of a nerve cell, along which impulses received from other cells at synapses are transmitted.
electron: A stable subatomic particle with a charge of negative electricity
friction: The resistance that one surface or object encounters when moving over another.
genetics: The study of heredity and the variation of inherited characteristics.
herbivore: An animal that feeds on plants.
isotope: Each of two or more forms of the same element that contain equal numbers of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
kinetics: The branch of chemistry or biochemistry concerned with measuring and studying the rates of reactions.
lithosphere: The rigid outer part of the earth, consisting of the crust and upper mantle.
molecule: A group of atoms bonded together, representing the smallest fundamental unit of a chemical compound.
nucleus: The positively charged central core of an atom, consisting of protons and neutrons and containing nearly all its mass.
osmosis: A process by which molecules of a solvent tend to pass through a semipermeable membrane from a less concentrated solution into a more concentrated one.
pathogen: A bacterium, virus, or other microorganism that can cause disease.
quantum: A discrete quantity of energy proportional in magnitude to the frequency of the radiation it represents.
radiation: The emission of energy as electromagnetic waves or as moving subatomic particles, especially high-energy particles which cause ionization.
symbiosis: Interaction between two different organisms living in close physical association, typically to the advantage of both.
taxonomy: The branch of science concerned with classification, especially of organisms; systematics.
universe: All existing matter and space considered as a whole; the cosmos.
vacuole: A space or vesicle within the cytoplasm of a cell, enclosed by a membrane and typically containing fluid.
velocity: The speed of something in a given direction.
wattage: A measure of electrical power expressed in watts.
chromosome: A threadlike structure of nucleic acids and protein found in the nucleus of most living cells, carrying genetic information.
mitochondria: An organelle found in large numbers in most cells, in which the biochemical processes of respiration and energy production occur.
photosynthesis: The process by which green plants and some other organisms use sunlight to synthesize nutrients from carbon dioxide and water.
thermodynamics: The branch of physical science that deals with the relations between heat and other forms of energy.
pneumonoultramicroscopicsilicovolcanocon: An invented word for a lung disease caused by inhaling very fine ash and sand dust; the longest English word.
deoxyribonucleic: A nucleic acid that is the main constituent of the chromosomes of all organisms (part of DNA).
electromagnetism: The interaction of electric currents or fields and magnetic fields
paleontology: The branch of science concerned with fossil animals and plants.
bioluminescence: The biochemical emission of light by living organisms such as fireflies and deep-sea fishes.
crystallography: The branch of science concerned with the structure and properties of crystals.
spectroscopy: The branch of science concerned with the investigation and measurement of spectra produced when matter interacts with or emits electromagnetic radiation.
neurotransmitter: A chemical substance that is released at the end of a nerve fiber by the arrival of a nerve impulse, causing the transfer of the impulse to another nerve fiber.
zoology: The scientific study of the behavior, structure, physiology, classification, and distribution of animals.
xylem: The vascular tissue in plants that conducts water and dissolved nutrients upward from the root.
phloem: The vascular tissue in plants that conducts sugars and other metabolic products downward from the leaves.
zygote: A diploid cell resulting from the fusion of two haploid gametes.
mycelium: The vegetative part of a fungus, consisting of a network of fine white filaments.
nebula: A cloud of gas and dust in outer space, visible in the night sky.
pulsar: A highly magnetized rotating neutron star that emits beams of electromagnetic radiation.
quasar: A massive and extremely remote celestial object emitting exceptionally large amounts of energy.
allele: One of two or more alternative forms of a gene
gamete: A mature haploid male or female germ cell that is able to unite with another of the opposite sex in sexual reproduction.
halogen: Any of the elements fluorine, chlorine, bromine, iodine, and astatine.
inertia: A property of matter by which it continues in its existing state of rest or uniform motion in a straight line, unless that state is changed by an external force.
joule: The SI unit of work or energy.
kelvin: The SI base unit of thermodynamic temperature.
atom: The basic building block of all matter.
cell: The basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.
gene: A unit of heredity that is transferred from a parent to offspring.
mars: The fourth planet from the sun in the solar system.
moon: The natural satellite of the earth, visible by reflected light from the sun.
star: A fixed luminous point in the night sky which is a large, remote incandescent body.
lung: Each of the pair of organs situated within the rib cage, into which air is drawn.
bone: Any of the pieces of hard, whitish tissue making up the skeleton.
mass: A coherent, typically large body of matter with no definite shape.
root: The part of a plant which attaches it to the ground or to a support.
seed: A flowering plant's unit of reproduction, capable of developing into another such plant.
time: The indefinite continued progress of existence and events in the past, present, and future.
watt: The SI unit of power, equivalent to one joule per second.
zinc: The chemical element of atomic number 30, a silvery-white metal.
acid: A chemical substance that neutralizes alkalis, dissolves some metals.
base: A substance capable of reacting with an acid to form a salt and water.
data: Facts and statistics collected together for reference or analysis.
heat: The quality of being hot; high temperature.
iron: A strong, hard magnetic silvery-gray metal, the chemical element of atomic number 26.
lava: Hot molten or semifluid rock erupted from a volcano or fissure.
nova: A star showing a sudden large increase in brightness and then slowly returning to its original state.
brain: An organ of soft nervous tissue contained in the skull of vertebrates.
earth: The planet on which we live; the world.
fluid: A substance that has no fixed shape and yields easily to external pressure.
force: Strength or energy as an attribute of physical action or movement.
light: The natural agent that stimulates sight and makes things visible.
magma: Hot fluid or semifluid material below or within the earth's crust.
metal: A solid material that is typically hard, shiny, malleable, fusible, and ductile
plant: A living organism of the kind exemplified by trees, shrubs, herbs, grasses, ferns, and mosses.
power: The rate of doing work, or the limit of physical or mental ability.
sound: Vibrations that travel through the air or another medium and can be heard.
space: A continuous area or expanse which is free, available, or unoccupied.
water: A colorless, transparent, odorless liquid that forms the seas, lakes, rivers, and rain.
virus: An infective agent that typically consists of a nucleic acid molecule in a protein coat.
asteroid: A rock orbiting the Sun that is smaller than a planet
aerodynamics: The study of the motion of air, particularly its interaction with a solid object
alveolar: Relating to the alveoli of the lungs
amethyst: A precious stone consisting of a violet or purple variety of quartz
antiform: A geological fold that is convex upward
analyzer: An instrument used for analyzing a substance
aphelion: The point in the orbit of a planet, asteroid, or comet at which it is furthest from the sun
aperture: An opening, hole, or gap; especially in an optical system
arachnid: An arthropod of the class Arachnida, such as a spider or scorpion
argon: The chemical element of atomic number 18, an inert gaseous element
asbestos: A highly heat-resistant fibrous silicate mineral that can be woven into fabrics
astrophysics: The branch of astronomy concerned with the physical nature of stars
attenuation: The reduction of the amplitude of a signal, electric current, or other oscillation
autosome: Any chromosome that is not a sex chromosome
avalanche: A mass of snow, ice, and rocks falling rapidly down a mountainside
axon: The long threadlike part of a nerve cell along which impulses are conducted
azimuth: The direction of a celestial object from the observer
bacteriophage: A virus that parasitizes a bacterium by infecting it and reproducing inside it
bandwidth: A range of frequencies within a given band
bifurcate: Divide into two branches or forks
bimetallic: Made of two different metals, often used in thermostats
biochemistry: The branch of science concerned with the chemical processes within living organisms
biomechanics: The study of the mechanical laws relating to the movement of living organisms
bionics: The study of mechanical systems that function like parts of living organisms
biosphere: The regions of the surface and atmosphere of the earth occupied by living organisms
bivalve: An aquatic mollusk that has a compressed body enclosed within a hinged shell
breccia: Rock consisting of angular fragments cemented together
bronchus: Any of the major air passages of the lungs that diverge from the windpipe
buoyancy: The upward force exerted by a fluid that opposes the weight of an immersed object
calculus: The branch of mathematics that deals with the finding and properties of derivatives and integrals
caldera: A large volcanic crater, typically one formed by a major eruption
calibration: The action or process of calibrating an instrument or experimental readings
calorimeter: An apparatus for measuring the amount of heat involved in a chemical reaction
cambrian: The first geological period of the Paleozoic Era
capacitance: The ability of a system to store an electric charge
capillary: Any of the fine branching blood vessels that form a network
carapace: The hard upper shell of a turtle, crustacean, or arachnid
catalysis: The acceleration of a chemical reaction by a catalyst
cathode: The negatively charged electrode by which electrons enter an electrical device
centrifuge: A machine with a rapidly rotating container that applies centrifugal force
cerebrum: The principal and most anterior part of the brain in vertebrates
chromatography: The separation of a mixture by passing it in solution or suspension through a medium
coagulation: The action or process of a liquid, especially blood, changing to a solid or semi-solid state
colloid: A mixture in which one substance of microscopically dispersed insoluble particles is suspended throughout another substance
conduction: The process by which heat or electricity is directly transmitted through a substance
conglomerate: A coarse-grained sedimentary rock composed of rounded fragments
convection: The movement caused within a fluid by the tendency of hotter and therefore less dense material to rise
cotyledon: An embryonic leaf in seed-bearing plants
craton: A large, stable block of the earth's crust forming the nucleus of a continent
cyclone: A system of winds rotating inward to an area of low atmospheric pressure
cytology: The branch of biology concerned with the structure and function of plant and animal cells
decibel: A unit used to measure the intensity of a sound
declination: The angular distance of a point north or south of the celestial equator
decomposition: The state or process of rotting; decay
dendrochronology: The science or technique of dating events, environmental change, and archaeological artifacts by using the characteristic patterns of annual growth rings in timber and tree trunks
denitrification: The loss or removal of nitrogen or nitrogen compounds
density: The degree of compactness of a substance
deposition: The geological process in which sediments, soil and rocks are added to a landform or land mass
desalination: The process of removing salt from seawater
dichotomy: A division or contrast between two things that are or are represented as being opposed or entirely different
diffraction: The process by which a beam of light or other system of waves is spread out as a result of passing through a narrow aperture
dilution: The action of making a liquid more dilute
diode: A semiconductor device with two terminals, typically allowing the flow of current in one direction only
diploid: Containing two complete sets of chromosomes, one from each parent
dispersion: The distinct phases of a mixture where one substance acts as the medium to hold another
distillation: The action of purifying a liquid by a process of heating and cooling
divergence: The process or state of diverging
dolomite: A translucent mineral consisting of a carbonate of calcium and magnesium
doppler: An increase (or decrease) in the frequency of sound, light, or other waves as the source and observer move toward (or away from) each other
ductility: The physical property of a material associated with the ability to be hammered thin or stretched into wire without breaking
dynamics: The branch of mechanics concerned with the motion of bodies under the action of forces
dynamo: A machine for converting mechanical energy into electrical energy; a generator
eccentricity: Deviation of a curve or orbit from circularity
eclipse: An obscuring of the light from one celestial body by the passage of another
ecology: The branch of biology that deals with the relations of organisms to one another and to their physical surroundings
ecosystem: A biological community of interacting organisms and their physical environment
ectoderm: The outermost layer of cells or tissue of an embryo in early development
effusion: An instance of giving off something such as a liquid, light, or smell
elasticity: The ability of an object or material to resume its normal shape after being stretched or compressed
electrode: A conductor through which electricity enters or leaves an object, substance, or region
electrolysis: Chemical decomposition produced by passing an electric current through a liquid or solution containing ions
electrolyte: A liquid or gel that contains ions and can be decomposed by electrolysis
element: Each of more than one hundred substances that cannot be chemically interconverted or broken down into simpler substances
embryology: The branch of biology and medicine concerned with the study of embryos and their development
emission: The production and discharge of something, especially gas or radiation
emulsion: A fine dispersion of minute droplets of one liquid in another in which it is not soluble or miscible
endemic: Regularly found among particular people or in a certain area
endocrine: Relating to or denoting glands which secrete hormones or other products directly into the blood
endoderm: The innermost layer of cells or tissue of an embryo in early development
entomology: The branch of zoology concerned with the study of insects
entropy: A thermodynamic quantity representing the unavailability of a system's thermal energy for conversion into mechanical work
epicenter: The point on the earth's surface vertically above the focus of an earthquake
epiglottis: A flap of cartilage at the root of the tongue, which is depressed during swallowing to cover the opening of the windpipe
epiphyte: A plant that grows on another plant but is not parasitic
equilibrium: A state in which opposing forces or influences are balanced
erosion: The process of eroding or being eroded by wind, water, or other natural agents
estuary: The tidal mouth of a large river, where the tide meets the stream
ethnology: The study of the characteristics of various peoples and the differences and relationships between them
eukaryote: An organism consisting of a cell or cells in which the genetic material is DNA in the form of chromosomes contained within a distinct nucleus
evaporation: The process of turning from liquid into vapor
exoskeleton: A rigid external covering for the body in some invertebrate animals
exothermic: Accompanied by the release of heat
algorithm: A set of rules to be followed in calculations
altitude: The height of an object or point in relation to sea level
amplitude: The maximum extent of a vibration or oscillation
anther: The part of a stamen that contains the pollen
aqueous: Of or containing water, typically as a solvent
aquifer: A body of permeable rock which can contain or transmit groundwater
artery: Blood vessels that deliver oxygen-rich blood
arthritis: Inflammation of one or more of your joints
arthropod: An invertebrate animal having an exoskeleton and a segmented body
assimilation: The absorption and digestion of food or nutrients
asymptote: A line that a curve approaches, as it heads towards infinity
beaker: A lipped cylindrical glass container for laboratory use
benthos: The flora and fauna found on the bottom of a sea or lake
binomial: An algebraic expression of the sum or the difference of two terms
boiling: The action of bringing a liquid to the temperature at which it bubbles and turns to vapor
carbohydrate: Any of a large group of organic compounds occurring in foods and living tissues
chloroplast: A plastid that contains chlorophyll and in which photosynthesis takes place
clone: An organism or cell produced asexually from one ancestor
compound: A thing that is composed of two or more separate elements
derivative: The rate of change of a function with respect to a variable
diffusion: The spreading of something more widely
distal: Situated away from the center of the body
dominant: A trait that will appear in the offspring if one of the parents contributes it
efferent: Conducted or conducting outward or away from something
epidermis: The outer layer of cells covering an organism
fault: An extended break in a rock formation
fertilization: The action or process of fertilizing an egg
focal: Relating to the center or main point of interest
foodweb: A system of interlocking and interdependent food chains
fulcrum: The point on which a lever rests or is supported
fusion: The process or result of joining two or more things together
geothermal: Relating to or produced by the internal heat of the earth
germination: The process by which a plant grows from a seed
gland: An organ in the human or animal body which secretes particular chemical substances
greenhouse: A glass building in which plants are grown
gymnosperm: A plant that has seeds unprotected by an ovary or fruit
hardwood: The wood from a broadleaved tree
heredity: The passing on of physical or mental characteristics genetically
heterotroph: An organism deriving its nutritional requirements from complex organic substances
hybrid: The offspring of two plants or animals of different species or varieties
immune: Resistant to a particular infection or toxin
incisor: A narrow-edged tooth at the front of the mouth
inference: A conclusion reached on the basis of evidence and reasoning
insect: A small arthropod animal that has six legs
insulator: A substance which does not readily allow the passage of heat or sound
kinetic: Relating to or resulting from motion
latent: Existing but not yet developed or manifest
liquid: A substance that flows freely but is of constant volume
lysosome: An organelle in the cytoplasm of eukaryotic cells containing degradative enzymes
machine: An apparatus using or applying mechanical power
melting: Make or become liquified by heating
meniscus: The curved upper surface of a liquid in a tube
nekton: Aquatic animals that are able to swim and move independently of water currents
nitrogen: A colorless, odorless unreactive gas that forms about 78 percent of the earth's atmosphere
nonmetal: An element or substance that is not a metal
ocean: A very large expanse of sea
ovary: A female reproductive organ in which ova or eggs are produced
ovule: The part of the ovary of seed plants that contains the female germ cell
oxidation: The process or result of oxidizing or being oxidized
parallel: Side by side and having the same distance continuously between them
parasite: An organism that lives in or on an organism of another species
peptide: A short chain of amino acids
perihelion: The point in the orbit of a planet, asteroid, or comet at which it is closest to the sun
pharynx: The membrane-lined cavity behind the nose and mouth
phase: A distinct period or stage in a process of change or forming part of something's development
phenotype: The set of observable characteristics of an individual
plate: Each of the several rigid pieces of the earth's lithosphere
pollen: A fine powdery substance consisting of microscopic grains
polymer: A substance that has a molecular structure consisting chiefly or entirely of a large number of similar units
population: All the inhabitants of a particular town, area, or country
potential: Latent qualities or abilities that may be developed
precipitate: Cause to happen suddenly, unexpectedly, or prematurely
predator: An animal that naturally preys on others
pressure: The continuous physical force exerted on or against an object
prey: An animal that is hunted and killed by another for food
producer: An organism, either a green plant or bacterium, which is part of the first level of a food chain
proton: A stable subatomic particle occurring in all atomic nuclei, with a positive electric charge
protozoa: A phylum or group of phyla that comprises the single-celled microscopic animals
pulley: A wheel with a grooved rim around which a cord passes
reaction: A process in which one or more substances are converted to one or more different substances
reproduction: The sexual or asexual process by which organisms generate new individuals of the same kind
reptile: A vertebrate animal of a class that includes snakes, lizards, crocodiles, turtles
resistance: The refusal to accept or comply with something
rotation: The action of rotating around an axis or center
series: A number of things, events, or people of a similar kind or related nature coming one after another
solute: The minor component in a solution, dissolved in the solvent
solution: A liquid mixture in which the minor component is uniformly distributed within the major component
speed: The rate at which someone or something is able to move or operate
spore: A minute, typically one-celled, reproductive unit capable of giving rise to a new individual
stamen: The male fertilizing organ of a flower
state: The particular condition that someone or something is in at a specific time
stigma: The part of a pistil that receives the pollen during pollination
stimulus: A thing or event that evokes a specific functional reaction in an organ or tissue
stomata: Any of the minute pores in the epidermis of the leaf or stem of a plant
stratosphere: The layer of the earth's atmosphere above the troposphere
sublimation: The transition of a substance directly from the solid to the gas phase
system: A set of connected things or parts forming a complex whole
tissue: Any of the distinct types of material of which animals or plants are made
topography: The arrangement of the natural and artificial physical features of an area
tornado: A mobile, destructive vortex of violently rotating winds
translation: The process of translating the sequence of a messenger RNA (mRNA) molecule to a sequence of amino acids
transpiration: The exhalation of water vapor through the stomata
trough: A long, narrow open receptacle, usually boxlike in shape
tsunami: A long high sea wave caused by an earthquake, submarine landslide, or other disturbance
ventricle: A hollow part or cavity in an organ
volcano: A mountain or hill having a crater or vent through which lava, rock fragments, hot vapor, and gas are being or have been erupted
volume: The amount of space that a substance or object occupies
wave: A periodic disturbance of the particles of a fluid
work: Activity involving mental or physical effort
